leadership.name: Scott Sharkey
leadership.background: Founder and Manager of the franchisor since April 2013 and predecessor since July 2003.

leadership.name: Michael Cohen
leadership.background: Joined in August 2018; previously President of Darby Litho in NYC since 1999.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees site selection and real estate processes.

leadership.name: Jamie Raney
leadership.role: Director of Franchise Support & Salon Operations
leadership.background: Joined in February 2022; previously Franchise Business Consultant for Regis Corporation.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs ongoing franchisee support and operational standards.

hiddenCosts.name: Inventory Control Minimum
hiddenCosts.description: Must maintain inventory of at least $4,500 wholesale dollars at all times.

hiddenCosts.description: Fee of $750 to $1,750 per person plus food, travel, and lodging.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved by arbitration and/or litigation only in Connecticut.

operationalRisks.title: Spouse Liability
operationalRisks.description: Spouse must sign a personal guarantee making them liable for financial obligations.

operationalRisks.title: Inventory Control Requirement
operationalRisks.description: Must maintain a minimum inventory of $4,500 at all times.

operationalRisks.title: High Turnover Rate
operationalRisks.description: 37 franchised outlets were transferred, terminated, reacquired, or ceased operations in the last 3 years.

extracted.conceptRationale: The concept is a specialized hair salon designed specifically for children and families.
extracted.staffingRationale: Salons require licensed stylists and on-site management to perform hair cutting services.

riskReasons: 2 high-severity operational tripwires disclosed — see the tripwires section.

notes: Royalty % not found; variable costs may be understated.

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $270,498 mid-point build-out, there is a $20,498 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $17,406/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: The concept is a specialized hair salon designed specifically for children and families.

benchmark.label: Beauty / personal care
benchmark.dominantRisk: Commission/booth-rent labor and chair utilization. Labor structure dominates economics.
considerations: Labor (commission or booth-rent) is the big line at ~35–50%; COGS on product is modest.
Verify chair/room utilization and stylist retention with Item 20 operators.
Confirm whether labor runs on commission, hourly, or booth rent — it changes the model entirely.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 8–18% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 3–4.3 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Beauty / personal care” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Taken from the FDD (~$5,500/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 8–18% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 35–50% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor's statements don't show the distress signals we screen for — positive net worth and a clean audit. Still worth a quick confirmation against current figures.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
